content.text: Review Gates for Claude System Designs

Review gates are synchronisation points in a Claude integration's development lifecycle where the integration must demonstrate it meets a defined set of criteria before work can proceed to the next phase. Gates serve a different purpose from the Design Review Board session: the DRB session is a one-time event where an architecture proposal is reviewed; gates are recurring checkpoints that enforce quality standards at each transition from concept to production.

A gate-based approach to Claude integration development has two effects. First, it concentrates review effort at the transitions where risk is highest — moving from architecture to implementation, and from development to production. Second, it prevents the accumulation of unresolved problems: a team that cannot pass Gate 1 cannot proceed to build, which means the problems surfaced at Gate 1 are fixed before they compound into implementation debt.

attrs.alt: Four-gate pipeline for Claude system designs: Gate 0 (concept), Gate 1 (architecture/DRB), Gate 2 (security), Gate 3 (production), with rejection causing return to design phase

content.text: The Four-Gate Model

Enterprise Claude integrations pass through four gates from concept to production. Each gate has a defined set of entry criteria (what must be true before the gate review begins), gate criteria (what must be demonstrated to pass), and exit artefacts (what documentation is produced as evidence of gate passage).
Gate 0: Concept Approval
Purpose

Gate 0 is a lightweight pre-investment check. Before a team invests in PoC development, they confirm that Claude is the appropriate solution for the problem they are trying to solve, that there is a business case for the investment, and that the scope of the PoC is bounded enough to produce useful signal within the allotted time and budget.
Gate 0 Criteria
Problem-solution fit:

The problem statement explains why Claude (a large language model) is the appropriate tool — not a simpler system. The integration team can articulate what specific capability of Claude (language understanding, reasoning, generation, document processing) addresses the problem.
Business case approved:
A business case document has been approved by the BU sponsor. The business case includes a measurable success metric (the north-star KPI) and a target value that constitutes success.
PoC scope defined:

The PoC scope document defines what will and will not be built during the PoC phase, the user group that will participate in the pilot, and the evaluation criteria that will determine whether the PoC proceeds to production.
Risk acknowledgment:

The team and BU sponsor have acknowledged the key risks of a Claude integration (cost unpredictability at early stage, quality variability, system prompt sensitivity) and confirmed they are acceptable for the PoC phase.
Gate 0 Process

Gate 0 is not a DRB session — it is a lightweight approval by the AI programme office or equivalent governance body, typically reviewed asynchronously. The gate package is a short document (3–5 pages) rather than a full submission package. The reviewing body approves or returns with questions within 5 business days.

GATE 0 SUBMISSION TEMPLATE

Integration name: [name]
BU Sponsor: [name, title]
PoC Lead: [name]
Submission date: YYYY-MM-DD

1. PROBLEM STATEMENT (max 200 words)
[What problem are you solving? Who is affected? What is the current solution,
and why is it insufficient?]

2. WHY CLAUDE (max 150 words)
[What specific LLM capability addresses this problem? Why is Claude the right
choice vs. a simpler rule-based or ML-based solution?]

3. NORTH-STAR KPI
Metric: [specific, measurable metric — e.g. "contract review cycle time"]
Baseline: [current value — e.g. "12.4 days"]
Target: [target value — e.g. "< 9 days by end of 3-month pilot"]
Measurement method: [how will you measure this?]

4. POC SCOPE
In scope: [what will be built]
Out of scope: [what will NOT be built during PoC]
Pilot user group: [who, how many, selection criteria]
PoC duration: [start date → end date]

5. KEY RISKS
[2–4 risks acknowledged by the BU sponsor as acceptable for the PoC phase]
Gate 1: Architecture Approval
Purpose

Gate 1 is the primary architecture gate. It is the moment at which the Design Review Board reviews the full integration design and decides whether it is sound enough to build. No material implementation work should begin before Gate 1 is passed.
Gate 1 Criteria
Criterion
Evidence Required
Reviewer
DRB decision: APPROVED or CONDITIONAL APPROVED with pre-build conditions resolved
DRB decision record
Architecture lead
All ADRs for this integration are in ACCEPTED status
ADR index with status for each ADR
Architecture lead
Architecture diagram is current and reviewed
Diagram reviewed as part of DRB package
Architecture lead
Cost projection at target scale is within approved budget ceiling
Cost model reviewed by DRB; BU sponsor confirmation
Operations lead
Security assessment submitted to DRB and no blocking security findings outstanding
Security section of DRB submission; security representative sign-off
Security representative
Operational readiness plan exists (runbook outline, monitoring plan)
Included in DRB submission; operations lead reviewed
Operations lead
Gate 1 Timing

Gate 1 must occur after PoC completion and before production build begins. The PoC exit criteria (a separate gate within the PoC phase) must be met before the Gate 1 submission is prepared — a PoC that has not met its exit criteria does not have the empirical data required for a sound Gate 1 submission.

The most common Gate 1 failure is submitting before the PoC exit criteria are met, with a speculative cost projection and an architecture diagram that reflects intent rather than validated design. A Gate 1 submission prepared from PoC data — actual cost per request, actual latency distribution, actual eval scores — produces a review that can be approved. A submission prepared from assumptions produces conditional approval at best.
Gate 2: Security and Compliance Review
Purpose

Gate 2 is an independent security and compliance review conducted after Gate 1 approval and before production deployment. It is separate from the DRB's security review at Gate 1 because the DRB reviews the design, while Gate 2 reviews the implementation — two different artefacts at two different stages of development.
Gate 2 Criteria
Penetration test completed:

A penetration test or security assessment has been conducted against the built integration (not the design). All critical and high-severity findings are resolved. Medium-severity findings have accepted remediation plans with timelines. The penetration test report is provided to the security representative.
Threat model reviewed:
The threat model from the architecture phase has been updated to reflect the actual implementation. Any threats that were not considered at architecture time are documented and assessed.
Data handling verification:

The data handling implementation matches the data handling ADR. Personal data is handled as documented. Retention periods are implemented. Audit logging is confirmed to be capturing the events specified in the security ADR.
Prompt injection assessment:

The integration has been assessed for prompt injection vulnerabilities — attempts by users to modify the system prompt or instruct the model to behave outside its design. The assessment methodology and results are documented.
Credential management confirmed:

All API credentials are stored in the approved secrets management system. No credentials are hardcoded or stored in environment variable files that could be committed to source control. A credential rotation procedure is documented and has been tested.
Gate 2 Timing

Gate 2 is conducted when the integration is functionally complete and in a staging environment that is representative of production. Running a penetration test against a partially built integration is not useful — the test would miss attack surfaces that do not yet exist. The gate is typically reached 2–4 weeks before the planned production deployment date.
Gate 3: Production Readiness
Purpose

Gate 3 is the final sign-off before production deployment. By this point the architecture is approved, the security review is complete, and the integration is built. Gate 3 confirms that the operational model is ready — the team that will operate the system has the tools, documentation, and training to do so from day one.
Gate 3 Criteria
Criterion
Evidence Required
Owner
Runbook complete and tested
Operations team member has followed every procedure end-to-end without asking for help from the PoC team
Operations lead
Monitoring and alerting live and tested
Dashboard URL showing live metrics; alert routing tested by triggering a test alert
Operations lead
Eval pipeline automated and baseline established
First automated eval run completed against the production build; baseline scores recorded
PoC team / Operations lead
Operations team trained on AI-specific procedures
Training completion record; ops team lead attests to readiness
Operations lead
Rollback procedure tested
A rollback to the prior version (or a canary deployment) has been executed in staging and confirmed successful
Operations lead
Capacity confirmed
API tier is sufficient for launch volume; rate limit headroom confirmed
Operations lead
Post-handoff support period agreed
PoC team availability confirmed for 6–8 weeks post-launch
PoC team lead
Gate Criteria Checklist Format
Each gate passage is recorded using a structured checklist that becomes part of the integration's governance record. The checklist is signed by the gate owner and stored alongside the ADR index.

GATE [N] PASSAGE RECORD
Integration: [name] | Version: [version]
Gate owner: [name, role] | Date: YYYY-MM-DD

CRITERIA                                            MET    EVIDENCE
[ ] [Criterion 1]                                   YES    [Link or description]
[ ] [Criterion 2]                                   YES    [Link or description]
[ ] [Criterion 3 — conditional]                     YES    [Condition satisfied as of YYYY-MM-DD]

OUTSTANDING ITEMS (none for APPROVED; conditions for CONDITIONAL)
  [item]: [resolution plan and owner]

GATE DECISION: APPROVED | CONDITIONAL | BLOCKED

Gate owner signature: _______________   Date: ___________
[Additional reviewer if required]: ___  Date: ___________

NEXT GATE
Gate [N+1] submission due: YYYY-MM-DD
Gate Exceptions

Occasionally a team will request an exception to gate criteria — permission to proceed despite not fully meeting a criterion. Gate exceptions must be approved by the architecture lead and documented. Undocumented exceptions are not exceptions — they are governance failures.
What can be excepted:
Non-blocking criteria with documented compensating controls, or criteria that are time-bound (e.g., pen test in progress at Gate 1 approval; must complete before Gate 2).
What cannot be excepted:
DRB APPROVED decision (Gate 1), critical security findings (Gate 2), untested runbook (Gate 3). These are not administrative criteria — they are substantive risk controls.
Exception record format:

The exception must state what criterion is not met, why the exception is justified, what compensating control is in place, and when the criterion will be fully met. The exception must be re-reviewed at the next gate.

Gate exceptions that are not remedied by the next gate review become chronic exceptions — they stop being exceptions and become the new baseline. An integration that goes live with an untested runbook as an 'exception' will still have an untested runbook at its first 90-day review if no one is tracking the exception to closure. The gate exception record is only valuable if the architecture lead reviews open exceptions at every gate cycle.
Lightweight Gate Process for Smaller Integrations

The four-gate model is calibrated for production integrations serving 100+ users with enterprise-level compliance requirements. For smaller integrations (internal tools, limited user population, lower risk profile), a two-gate variant is appropriate:
Lightweight Gate
Corresponds to
What Is Retained
What Is Simplified
Lightweight Gate 0
Gates 0 and 1 combined
DRB review; ADR requirement; cost projection; security assessment
Single submission package covers both; no separate business case document required
Lightweight Gate 1
Gates 2 and 3 combined
Security review; runbook; monitoring
Security review may be self-assessment rather than penetration test; runbook may be abbreviated

The lightweight gate process applies when all of the following are true: fewer than 50 users; no regulated data categories; no customer-facing interface; cost projection under $5,000/month. When any of these conditions is no longer true (user population grows, data category changes), the integration is promoted to the full four-gate process and the outstanding gate criteria for the current phase must be met within 60 days.
Integrating Gates with Existing Development Processes

The four-gate model is designed to complement, not replace, existing software development lifecycle processes. Most enterprise teams already have a project lifecycle (agile sprints, stage-gate project management, or a waterfall process) and an existing governance structure (architecture review, security review, change advisory board). The gate model maps to these existing structures rather than running parallel to them.
Mapping Gates to Agile Delivery
For teams using sprint-based delivery, the gate checkpoints are triggered by phase transitions rather than calendar milestones:
Gate 0
occurs before the PoC sprint begins — typically in the project initiation sprint when the team is scoping the work.
Gate 1
occurs after the PoC retrospective and before the first production build sprint begins — typically at the sprint review where PoC results are presented.
Gate 2
occurs after the hardening sprint (when security findings are addressed) and before the deployment sprint.
Gate 3
occurs in the deployment sprint, before the production deployment task is scheduled. The gate is a deploy condition, not a post-deploy review.

In practice, the gate submission and review can be run in parallel with the sprint that prepares the artefacts. Gate 1 review can be scheduled for the sprint review meeting of the PoC sprint — the team presents their PoC results to the DRB rather than a separate internal retrospective. This approach avoids the overhead of a separate gate review event and integrates the governance checkpoint into the existing sprint cadence.
Mapping Gates to Stage-Gate Project Management
For organisations using a stage-gate or phase-gate project management framework, the Claude integration gates slot directly into the existing stage-gate structure:
Existing Stage Gate
Claude Integration Gate
Additional Criteria Beyond Standard Gate
Idea/Concept Gate
Gate 0
Claude fit assessment; LLM-specific risk acknowledgment
Development Gate (entry to build)
Gate 1
DRB decision; ADR status; cost projection
Quality Gate (pre-test)
Gate 2
Prompt injection assessment; Claude-specific security controls
Release Gate (pre-production)
Gate 3
Runbook tested; eval pipeline automated; AI-specific ops training

When an organisation has an existing release gate process (e.g., a Change Advisory Board approval), Gate 3 does not replace that process — it adds Claude-specific criteria to the existing gate. The CAB approval and Gate 3 sign-off can be combined into a single review event, with the Gate 3 checklist submitted as supporting documentation for the CAB request.
Common Gate Failure Patterns and Prevention

The same failure patterns recur across organisations that adopt gate-based governance for Claude integrations. Understanding these patterns in advance allows a programme to design its gate process to prevent them.
Failure Pattern
How It Manifests
Prevention
PoC-to-production rush
Team passes Gate 1 before completing PoC exit criteria; architecture is based on assumptions rather than PoC data
Gate 1 submission template includes a required field 'PoC exit criteria met: YES/NO' and date; gate owner does not accept a submission that is dated before the PoC retrospective
Security gate treated as checkbox
Gate 2 is passed with a self-assessment ('no security concerns identified') rather than an actual penetration test or threat model review
Gate 2 criteria specify 'penetration test completed' not 'security review completed'; self-assessment does not satisfy the criterion for integrations above the lightweight gate threshold
Runbook theatre
Gate 3 passes with a runbook that was never tested end-to-end; the operations team used it once in staging and declared it 'tested'
Gate 3 criterion requires 'operations team member who was NOT involved in writing the runbook has followed every procedure without asking for help'; the gate owner checks who conducted the test
Exception creep
Initial exception ('pen test will complete in 2 weeks') becomes a 3-month-old open item never remedied
All exceptions have a mandatory resolution date; architecture lead reviews open exceptions at each bi-weekly DRB session; exceptions not resolved by the review date trigger an escalation
Gate orphaning
Integration built and deployed before Gate 1 is submitted ('we'll do the gate review later'); the DRB reviews a system that is already in production
CI/CD pipeline has a gate status check; deploying to production without a Gate 3 record triggers an alert to the architecture lead and is blocked in the automated deployment workflow
Gate Metrics and Continuous Improvement
The gate process itself should be monitored and improved. Tracking gate metrics across the integration portfolio reveals patterns that indicate process health or dysfunction.
Gate 1 first-pass approval rate:

The percentage of Gate 1 submissions that are APPROVED on first review (without returning to CONDITIONAL or REJECTED). A rate below 60% suggests that teams are submitting before their designs are mature, or that the pre-submission coaching process is not effective.
Time from Gate 0 to Gate 1:

The time between concept approval and architecture approval, averaged across integrations. This measures the PoC cycle time. If the average is longer than the programme's target PoC duration, investigate whether teams are staying in PoC phase too long or struggling to complete PoC exit criteria.
Gate 2 finding severity distribution:

The distribution of security finding severities across Gate 2 reviews. A high proportion of critical findings at Gate 2 suggests that security review is not being integrated into the design process (Gate 1 and architecture phase) — critical findings should be caught earlier.
Gate 3 exception count:

The number of open exceptions at Gate 3 sign-off, across integrations. A programme target of zero exceptions at Gate 3 is aspirational; in practice, exceptions exist. But if the average Gate 3 has 2+ open exceptions, the gate is not functioning as a real checkpoint.
Post-production incident rate by gate history:

Track whether integrations that had a smoother gate history (fewer exceptions, fewer conditional approvals, shorter gate cycle times) have a lower post-production incident rate. This is the ultimate validation of the gate process: does it predict operational quality?

The gate process is not the goal — the goal is Claude integrations that work reliably in production and meet their business objectives. If the gate data shows that integrations with smooth gate histories have better operational outcomes, the gate process is earning its overhead. If there is no correlation, the gate criteria may need revision. The gates are a measurement system for the integration development process; they should be calibrated against the outcomes they are supposed to predict.
Summary

Review gates structure the Claude integration lifecycle into four checkpoints — concept, architecture, security, and production readiness — each with defined criteria that must be met before the next phase begins. Gate 0 confirms that Claude is the right tool and the business case is sound. Gate 1 confirms that the architecture is approved by the DRB and all ADRs are accepted. Gate 2 confirms that the built integration is secure and the implementation matches the approved design. Gate 3 confirms that the operational team is ready to run the system in production from day one.

Gates work because they are enforced: a team that cannot meet Gate 1 criteria cannot proceed to build. The gate exception process provides a controlled way to handle situations where full criteria cannot be met on the planned timeline, while ensuring that exceptions are documented, tracked, and remedied. Over time, the gate passage records accumulate into an evidence base that demonstrates governance rigour — useful for compliance audits, operational reviews, and onboarding new team members to the integration's history.
